memoryArtifacts.description: Origin Displayed: Scottish - Spelling variations of this family name include: Abraham, Abram, Abrams, Abrahams and others. First found in Balfeth, in Scotland, in 1163, where Adam Abraham, Bishop of Dunblain, held extensive lands. Some of the first settlers of this family name or some of its variants were: John and Thomas Abram who were among the first settlers in North America, settling in Virginia in 1635 and 1653 respectively; William Abrams settled in Maryland in 1733. (From www.HouseOfNames.com Archives copyright  2000 - 2009) This long-established surname is of early medieval English and Christian origin. It is either locational from the village of Abram, in the parish of Wigan, or it is a form of the Hebrew male given name "Abraham", meaning "father of a multitude ". In Britain, where the name is from this source, it was given by returning Crusaders to their sons to commemorate their visit to the Holy Land. The 1086 Domesday Book refers to Abraham, the priest, (in London), whilst in 1170 Abraham de Stradtuna was noted in the Documents relating to the Danelaw, Lincolnshire. The name as a surname was never Jewish in origin, the Jews being banned from Britain until 1655. As a baptismal name 'Abraham and Abram' was used by the non-conformists of the 18th century. In Wales Abram was a popular christian name, and is still occasionally found there. Early examples of the recordings include John Abraam in the Hundred Rolls of Bedfordshire in 1273, and Gilbert de Abraam in London in 1461. Later recordings include William Abram of Sephton, Lancashire, in 1617, and Sarah Abram, the daughter of Andrew and Gillian Abram, who was christened at St. Mary Abbots, Kensington, London, on 11 November 1646. The Coat of Arms most associated with the name is a shield lozengy gold and red, on a black chief the sun in his splendor, gold, the Crest being a cap of maintenance decorated with a plume of ostrich feathers, all proper. The first recorded spelling of the family name is shown to be that of Robert Abram, which was dated 1252, in the "Chartulary of the Monastery of Ramsey", Huntingdonshire, during the reign of King Henry 3rd, known as "The Frenchman", 1216 - 1272. Surnames became necessary when governments introduced personal taxation. In England this was known as Poll Tax. Throughout the centuries, surnames in every country have continued to "develop" often leading to astonishing variants of the original spelling.  Copyright: Name Origin Research www.surnamedb.com 1980 - 2014.

zh.lifeSummary: 當William Abraham於1543年11月19日之前出生在英國藍卡爾吉可哈姆時，他的父親Richard Abraham28歲，母親Judith Woodward23歲。他於1564年1月20日與Elizabeth Cowbron在英國英格蘭藍卡爾吉可哈姆結婚。他們至少是1個兒子和1個女兒的父母。他去世於1569年8月在他的家鄉，埋葬在英國藍卡爾吉可哈姆。

zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当William Abraham于1543年11月19日之前出生在英国藍卡爾吉可哈姆时，他的父亲Richard Abraham28岁，母亲Judith Woodward23岁。他于1564年1月20日与Elizabeth Cowbron在英国英格兰藍卡爾吉可哈姆结婚。他们至少是1个儿子和1个女儿的父母。他去世于1569年8月在他的家乡，埋葬在英国藍卡爾吉可哈姆。

nameMeanings.name: Abraham
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Mathai, Mohan, Raju, Saji, Prasad, Anil, Babu, Biju, Leela, Sajan, Shibu, Suma.

English, French, Spanish, German, Slovenian, Ethiopian, and Assyrian/Chaldean; Hungarian (Ábrahám), Slovak (mainly Abrahám, also Ábrahám), Czech (also Abrahám): from the Hebrew personal name ʾAbraham (modern spelling Avraham), borne by the Biblical patriarch revered by Jews as the founding father of the Jewish people (Genesis 11–25) and by Muslims as founder of all the Semitic peoples, both Hebrew and Arab (compare Ibrahim ). The original name of the Biblical patriarch was probably Abram , meaning ‘high father’ (from ab father, ram ‘exalted’), while its later form is explained in Genesis 17:5 as being derived from Hebrew ab hamon goyim ‘father of a multitude of nations’. It was widely used as a personal name among Christians as well as Jews in the Middle Ages. The name Abraham is also found among Christians in southern India, but since South Indians traditionally do not have hereditary surnames, the southern Indian name was in most cases registered as such only after immigration of its bearers to the US. As a surname of Hungarian origin Abraham is also found in Romania. — Note: Since Ethiopians do not have hereditary surnames, the Ethiopian name was registered as such only after immigration of its bearers to the US.
Irish: adoption of the English name (see 1 above) as an equivalent of Gaelic Mac an Bhreitheamhan ‘son of the judge’. See McBroom and compare Breheny .

variants.name: Abrahams
meanings: English, Dutch, and Jewish (Ashkenazic): patronymic from the personal name   Abraham  .

variants.name: Ibrahim
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Arabic/Muslim Ibrahim, Mohamed, Hassan, Ahmed, Mohammad, Ashraf, Ali, Samir, Ismail, Mohammed, Abdul, Ahmad.

Arabic: from the personal name Ibrāhīm, Arabic form of Abraham . In Islam, Ibrāhīm is identified as a prophet, the ancestor of all the Semitic peoples, both Hebrew and Arab, and the father of Ismāʿīl (see Ismail ) and Isḥāq (see Ishaq ). Bearers of this surname are both Muslims and Christians. Compare Ebrahim .

variants.name: Abrahamson
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Scandinavian Erik, Aagot, Algot, Anders, Eskil, Lars, Thora.
Americanized form (and a rare Swedish variant) of Swedish Abrahamsson: patronymic from the personal name Abraham .
Americanized form of Norwegian, Danish, and North German   Abrahamsen  , a cognate of 1 above.

variants.name: Abrajan
meanings: Hispanic (mainly Mexico): variant of   Abraham  .

variants.name: Abrahamsen
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Scandinavian Erik, Alf, Oddvar, Sven, Thor, Lars, Lief, Per.
Norwegian, Danish, Dutch, and North German: patronymic from the personal name   Abraham  . Compare   Abrahamson  .

variants.name: Abramo
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Salvatore, Angelo, Carmelo, Giuseppe, Guido, Luigi, Nunzio, Sal, Salvator, Saverio, Silvio.
Italian: from the personal name Abramo (see Abraham ).

variants.name: Abeles
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Jewish Aryeh, Mort.
Jewish (Ashkenazic): patronymic from Abele, a pet form of the Yiddish personal name Abe (from Hebrew ab ‘father’ or from the personal name Abraham ) + Yiddish possessive -s.
English: variant of Abel with possessive or excrescent -s.

variants.name: Abrahamian
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Armenian Armen, Sarkis, Harmik, Seta, Zaven, Adrine, Ara, Avadis, Bedros, Garnik, Hrach, Hrand.
Armenian: patronymic from the Biblical personal name   Abraham  . It is a variant of eastern Armenian   Abrahamyan   and western Armenian   Aprahamian  , found mainly in the US, Iran, and Lebanon.

variants.name: Abrahamyan
meanings: Armenian: patronymic from the eastern Armenian Biblical personal name   Abraham  . Compare   Abrahamian  ,   Abramyan  , and   Aprahamian  .

variants.name: Abram
meanings: English, German, Dutch, Polish, Slovenian, Croatian, and Jewish; Hungarian (Ábrám): from a shortened form of the Biblical personal name Abraham .

English: habitational name from a place near Manchester, recorded as Adburgham in the 12th century, Abraham in the late 14th century, and Abram in the 15th. The placename is from the Old English female personal name Ēadburg + hām ‘homestead’, meaning ‘Ēadburg's homestead’.

nameMeanings.name: William
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably the most successful of all the Old French names of Germanic origin that were introduced to England by the Normans. It is derived from Germanic wil ‘will, desire’ + helm ‘helmet, protection’. The fact that it was borne by the Conqueror himself does not seem to have inhibited its favour with the ‘conquered’ population: in the first century after the Conquest it was the commonest male name of all, and not only among the Normans. In the later Middle Ages it was overtaken by John , but continued to run second to that name until the 20th century, when the picture became more fragmented.

historicEvents.title: Book of Common Prayer
historicEvents.description: Book of Common Prayer, a product of the English Reformation, was published in 1549 for assistance in the administration of the sacraments and other rites and ceremonies of the church according to the use of the Church of England. The book outlined morning, evening, and communion prayers and orders for baptism and marriage, thus making England a truly Protestant state.

historicEvents.title: Act of Uniformity
historicEvents.description: The Act of Uniformity was passed by the Parliament of England and required all people to go to church once a week. The consequence of not attending church was a fine of 12 pence, which was a considerable amount for a poor person.

historicEvents.title: State Lottery
historicEvents.description: A State Lottery was recorded in 1569. The tickets were sold at St. Paul's Cathedral in London.

en.lifeSummary: When William Abraham was born before 19 November 1543, in Kirkham, Lancashire, England, his father, Richard Abraham, was 28 and his mother, Judith Woodward, was 23. He married Elizabeth Cowbron on 20 January 1564, in Kirkham, Lancashire, England, United Kingdom. They were the parents of at least 1 son and 1 daughter. He died in August 1569, in his hometown, and was buried in Kirkham, Lancashire, England.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque William Abraham est né avant 19 novembre 1543, à Kirkham, Lancashire, Angleterre, son père, Richard Abraham, avait 28 ans et sa mère, Judith Woodward, avait 23 ans. Il a épousé Elizabeth Cowbron le 20 janvier 1564, à Kirkham, Lancashire, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni. Ils ont eu au moins 1 fils et 1 fille. Il est décédé en août 1569, dans sa ville natale, et a été enterré à Kirkham, Lancashire, Angleterre.

de.lifeSummary: Als William Abraham vor 19. November 1543 in Kirkham, Lancashire, England geboren wurde, war sein Vater Richard Abraham 28 und seine Mutter Judith Woodward 23. Er hat am 20. Januar 1564 in Kirkham, Lancashire, England, United Kingdom Elizabeth Cowbron geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 1 Sohn und 1 Tochter. Er starb im August 1569 in seinem Geburtsort und wurde in Kirkham, Lancashire, England begraben.

it.lifeSummary: Quando William Abraham è nato il prima del 19 novembre 1543 nel Kirkham, Lancashire, England, il suo padre, Richard Abraham, aveva 28 anni e il suo madre, Judith Woodward, aveva 23 anni. Lui sposato Elizabeth Cowbron il 20 gennaio 1564, nel Kirkham, Lancashire, Inghilterra, Regno Unito. Erano genitori di almeno 1 figlio e 1 figlia. Morì nel agosto 1569, nel sua cittadina, e fu sepolto a Kirkham, Lancashire, England.

ja.lifeSummary: William Abrahamが1543年11月19日以前にイギリスランカシュアイアキルクハムで生まれた時，父Richard Abrahamは28歳，母Judith Woodwardは23歳であった。1564年1月20日にイギリスイギリスランカシュアイアキルクハムでElizabeth Cowbronと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも1人の息子と1人の娘の親であった。彼は1569年8月に故郷で死去し，イギリスランカシュアイアキルクハムにて埋葬された。

ko.lifeSummary: William Abraham(이)가 1543년 11월 19일 이전 영국 랭카실르 커카에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Richard Abraham(은)는 28세였고, 그의 어머니 Judith Woodward(은)는 23세였다. 그는 1564년 1월 20일 영국 잉글랜드 랭카실르 커카에서 Elizabeth Cowbron(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 한 명의 아들과 한 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 1569년 8월 그의 고향에서 사망했으며 영국 랭카실르 커카에 묻혔다.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда William Abraham родился перед 19 ноябрь 1543 г. в местности кирхам, ланкашир, Англия, его отцу по имени Richard Abraham было 28 лет, а матери, которую звали Judith Woodward, 23. Он женился на женщине по имени Elizabeth Cowbron 20 января 1564 г. в местности кирхам, ланкашир, Англия, Великобритания. У них был по крайней мере 1 сын и 1 дочь. Он умер август 1569 г. в его родном городе и был похоронен в местности кирхам, ланкашир, Англия.
